artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick family health + pool update

Hi Mom,

Finally grabbing a quiet half hour between Age Group Zoom dryland and getting Chloe into the bath. Thought I’d send a proper update instead of those half-finished texts.

First, about Dad and Harbour City. Before he even thinks about going back to the masters group there, I really want to know if QEII ever confirmed whether his pneumonia back in May 2020 was actually tied to that Legionella scare at the Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre. You remember – he ended up in QEII for three days after that Thursday night masters swim, and then the facility manager shut things down to deal with the air-quality issue. The club is planning to be back in the main pool at 1250 Waterfront Drive, but I’m pushing them to show that the investigation is properly wrapped up.

On top of that, Dad’s had a rough few years – the mild stroke at the Halifax Infirmary in March 2020, then the six‑day stay at QEII in February 2021 with COVID pneumonia. His respirologist there was pretty blunt with us about indoor pool decks. So when parents ask why I’m so strict about masks and screening, I usually tell them it’s because of what we’ve lived through with him.

Liam and Chloe are okay. Liam’s asthma has been more stable since the IWK visit last September, but I still need to be home by 8 p.m. on my coaching nights to do his meds because Emily’s usually on at QEII. Chloe’s still happily avoiding swim practice and covering the apartment in glitter and piano books.

How are you and Dad managing in Bedford this week? Any more trouble with his arthritis? I’m thinking of biking over Saturday afternoon if practice doesn’t run too late.

Love,
Michael

Title: Late-night planning brain dump – Phase 3 + life stuff

I need to get this all out of my head before morning practice.

Pools:
- Main base is still Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre (1250 Waterfront Drive). If the city closes it again like March 15–June 1, 2020 after that COVID exposure tied to Liam’s teammate’s dad (Mark Delaney, who ended up in the QEII COVID unit on May 3, 2020), I’ll lean on the Canada Games Centre GM about shifting early lanes there.
- I’ve tentatively booked two late-evening lanes at Dalhousie for September 2020 so I can split off the older teens who can’t seem to handle distancing – including the two boys whose parents came in on June 10 about the vaping in the Coburg Road parking lot.
- I also want some warm‑water therapy tank time at the Dal pool in August so I can demo technique without wrecking my back. Dr. Singh at CBI Physio warned me after the Halifax Infirmary MRI that the L4–L5 disc herniation from January 2020 is not something to mess with.

Logistics gaps:
- We’re still waiting on a proper written plan from the facility about how they’ll clean the changerooms between user groups. For now I’m basically assuming they’ll handle it the same way they did pre‑COVID, which isn’t ideal but I don’t have leverage.
- Need to keep all this straight in TeamUnify and Kinduct without living on the laptop 24/7.

Family:
- Don’t overload Sarah with Chloe drop‑offs at Sir Charles Tupper; she already cut IWK shifts once when pools reopened.
- Block evenings for Liam’s asthma meds.

Sleep now. Revisit after coffee.

metadata.title: Late-night planning brain dump – Phase 3 + life stuff

artifacts.content:
Subject: How we’re actually handling communication right now

Hi Megan,

Figured I’d write this out since everything’s been so piecemeal.

To be honest, our communication plan with families is still pretty informal. Most weeks I just end up firing off last‑minute emails or TeamUnify texts once Laura at Harbour City tells me what the pool can actually give us. I know we’ve talked about doing regular “Monday update” bulletins, but with schedules changing every few days it feels impossible to plan that far ahead.

I’m still using the BCC list I set up back in early May 2020, after Mark Delaney (Liam’s teammate’s dad) landed in the COVID unit at QEII on May 3. In that May 5 email I told everyone we were pausing in‑person dryland for 14 days and that all the Return to Swimming stuff would come through that channel. It’s clunky, but at least the families who’ve had to quarantine don’t miss updates if they can’t make the Zoom calls.

For the more anxious families, I’m trying to follow up individually. After our June 10 town hall (7 p.m.) I’ve got a note to call Ana Rodrigues – Chloe’s teammate’s mom – at her place off Gottingen. When we talked on May 28 she said she’s worried about her asthma and her permanent residency application if she gets sick and misses shifts at Northwood. She doesn’t like asking questions on Zoom, so I’ll walk her through the Phase 3 plan one‑on‑one.

If you see other parents who seem lost or stressed, flag them for me so I can reach out directly.

Thanks for hanging in through the chaos,
Michael

metadata.subject: How we’re actually handling communication right now

artifacts.content:
Title: Thursday – Physio + evening coverage

Time: Thursday, 6:30–8:00 p.m.
Location: Halifax Sports Medicine Clinic / Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre
Attendees: Michael, physiotherapist; Sarah covering Junior B/Development

Notes:

- Weekly physiotherapy and follow‑up at Halifax Sports Medicine Clinic for chronic right shoulder impingement and lower‑back disc issues. Both flared badly after I tried to demonstrate full butterfly sets for the Senior group on August 19, 2024. Need to bring MRI report from Halifax Infirmary (L4–L5 herniation, January 2020) and the latest notes from Dr. Singh at CBI Physiotherapy.

- Confirm with physio what I can safely demo on deck going forward – especially starts/turns in the warm‑water therapy tank at Dalhousie University pool if we keep those low‑impact technique sessions on Tuesday/Thursday mornings in August.

- Sarah Thompson is the designated on‑deck lead for any late‑evening Development or Junior B practices after November 1, 2024. She’ll cover supervision ratios while I’m at physio so I’m not tempted to stay on deck and make the shoulder worse.

- Remind Sarah that Liam’s asthma meds are on a strict 8:00 p.m. schedule since the IWK ER visit on September 12, 2024, so I’ll be bolting home right after physio on Mondays/Wednesdays/Fridays and can’t extend Senior or Age Group sessions beyond 90 minutes.

- Quick text to Emily at QEII to confirm her shift end; if she’s stuck on the unit, I can’t be late.

- Longer term: look at trimming my own masters swimming through March 2025. Between Dalplex 5:00–7:00 a.m. cleaning shifts to pay down that $4,800 line of credit from Feb 2021 and evening physio, something has to give.

metadata.title: Thursday – Physio + evening coverage
metadata.location: Halifax Sports Medicine Clinic / Harbour City Community Aquatic Centre

artifacts.content:
Subject: Gaps before we ramp up Phase 3

Hi Karen,

I’ve been trying to get all the loose ends in one place before we crank up Phase 3 next month.

Big picture, I think the training cohorts and lane assignments are solid, and Laura at Harbour City seems on board. Where I’m less confident is how we’re getting information to certain families. We’ve got the TeamUnify blasts and the Zoom town halls, but there are a handful of parents who rarely open email or check the app. Right now, if I’m honest, we haven’t really figured out how to reach them with the full COVID procedures. My default has just been to catch them at the first practice and talk them through the main points on deck, which isn’t ideal.

On top of that, I still haven’t actually put the Phase 3 protocols into a polished document for parents. I’ve got the guts of it in my notes and in emails to the board, but nothing I’m proud to send out club‑wide yet. Given how busy things are, my instinct has been to explain most of it verbally at that first session back and then follow up in writing once the dust settles.

Complicating things, my days are pretty chopped up this fall. I’m blocking weekday mornings for Sarah’s mediation stuff at Nova Scotia Family Court and calls with her lawyer at McInnes Cooper, so I’m trying to keep all parent meetings about protocols on Zoom in the evenings through at least January 2025.

If you have ideas for a simple way to plug the communication gaps without drowning everyone in emails, I’m all ears.

Thanks,
Michael

metadata.subject: Gaps before we ramp up Phase 3

artifacts.content:
Title: Coaching constraints + money reality – reminder to self

- Evening blocks for Senior and Age Group need to stay capped at 90 minutes through at least October 2024. I have to be home by 8:00 p.m. on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays to do Liam’s asthma and allergy meds since his IWK ER visit on Sept 12, 2024. Emily’s evening shifts at QEII mean she can’t reliably cover those doses.

- From November 1, 2024 onward, Sarah is the on‑deck lead for late Development and Junior B so I can get to my weekly Halifax Sports Medicine Clinic appointments for the shoulder impingement and lower‑back discs (worse since the August 19, 2024 butterfly heroics with Seniors). Don’t be a martyr – let her run the sets.

- No optional early‑morning masters sessions starting before 6:30 a.m. through March 2025. I’m still doing 5:00–7:00 a.m. cleaning shifts at Dalplex Fitness Centre on Mondays and Thursdays to keep up with payments on that $4,800 line of credit we took out in Feb 2021 when the club couldn’t pay me for three months during the first shutdown. Masters will survive with slightly later starts.

- Communication: I still haven’t sent a clean, formal write‑up of all the Phase 3 safety protocols. So far I’ve just hit the main points verbally with swimmers at practice and in quick chats with parents on deck. Need to carve out a Sunday afternoon to write something families can refer back to.

- Don’t forget Dad’s follow‑ups at QEII after the February 2021 COVID pneumonia and the earlier stroke at the Infirmary – coordinate visits with Sarah so one of us can always drive them from Bedford.

Breathe. One thing at a time.

metadata.title: Coaching constraints + money reality – reminder to self

artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching up + why I sound like a broken record on safety

Hey Sarah,

Kids are finally in bed, Emily’s on nights at QEII, and I’m procrastinating writing tomorrow’s Senior set, so you’re getting a ramble.

I was thinking today about how much our own mess has shaped how I talk to the swim families. In the March 15, 2021 safety email I sent to all the Harbour City parents, I mentioned Dad’s six days at QEII in February 2021 with COVID‑related pneumonia and how his respirologist basically scared me straight about indoor air and pool decks. Then there was his mild stroke at the Halifax Infirmary in March 2020, plus that earlier three‑day pneumonia stay after the Legionella scare at Harbour City in May 2020. No wonder I’m the mask police.

I even used Liam as an example in the August 18, 2022 “Safety & Health Protocols” PDF – remember when he woke up with a sore throat and mild cough on August 10 before his 6:30 a.m. practice at Centennial Pool? I wrote that if a kid feels like that, they stay home and parents email me so I can log it in TeamUnify and warn lane‑mates if needed.

And then there’s your stuff – you quarantined alone for 10 days in that tiny Quinpool Road apartment last April after the COVID exposure at the HRM offices, and now you’re tied up in Nova Scotia Family Court mediation since June 2024, juggling custody meetings and calls with McInnes Cooper. I’ve been telling parents that’s why we’re sticking to Zoom, email, and app notifications instead of on‑deck meetings for the first 6–8 weeks of any new phase.

Part of me worries I’m oversharing, but it seems to help them understand why I’m so firm.

Call me when you get a breather.

Love,
M.

metadata.subject: Catching up + why I sound like a broken record on safety

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Reynolds <m.reynolds@harbourcityaquatics.ca>
To: Dr. Harris <[redacted]@iwk.nshealth.ca>
Cc: Emily Reynolds <[redacted]@iwk.nshealth.ca>
Date: May 13, 2022
Subject: Follow-up on guidance for immunocompromised swimmer

Hi Dr. Harris,

Thank you again for taking the time on the evening of May 9, 2022 to speak with me about one of our swimmers who is immunocompromised following leukemia treatment. I really appreciate you spending roughly 40 minutes walking me through what to emphasize with the family and how to frame our pool-deck expectations more broadly.

After our call, I spoke with my spouse Emily (she was just finishing her IWK Health Centre shift) to make sure I understood the terminology correctly and that we were explaining the masking and symptom-screening expectations in a way that wouldn’t alarm the parents but would still be very clear. As I mentioned, our pool deck can get crowded, and I wanted to be sure we were aligning with what you’d recommend for a child in that situation.

On May 12, 2022, I sent our club-wide “Safety Snapshot” email to all Harbour City Aquatics families. I referenced an anonymized version of that family’s situation—no names or identifying details—simply as an example of why we’re asking everyone to respect the deck masking rules, keep their distance in the stands, and be honest on our wellness check-ins. I also noted that I’ve been doing one-on-one evening calls since May 2022 with parents who are under extra stress, so they know they can reach out directly to me if they’re unsure about bringing their child to practice.

If you see anything in that approach that you’d tweak, I’d be grateful for your feedback.

Best,
Michael James Reynolds
Head Coach & Program Director
Harbour City Aquatics Swim Club, Halifax

metadata.subject: Follow-up on guidance for immunocompromised swimmer

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Reynolds <m.reynolds@harbourcityaquatics.ca>
To: Senior Group – Harbour City Aquatics <senior@harbourcityaquatics.ca>
Date: September 5, 2023
Subject: Quick recap of today’s deck briefing at Dalplex

Hi all,

Thanks for the focus at Dalplex this afternoon. I wanted to put in writing a couple of the key points from the safety portion of our deck briefing on September 5, 2023 so there’s no confusion.

As I said on deck, if you have symptoms, you stay home and you tell us. To underline that this isn’t just a rule for swimmers, I shared my own experience from November 2020, when I was coaching a masters group at another club. I tested positive on a PCR test at the Halifax Infirmary midway through a training cycle. As soon as I got the result, I cancelled a full week of my own masters practices and immediately notified my then-employer and the 14 swimmers I’d coached that week by email so they could follow Public Health guidance.

I’ve also summarized that example in our written safety FAQ under “What happens if a coach gets COVID?” because I want you to see that transparency and quick communication are non-negotiable, even when it’s inconvenient. Missing a few practices is frustrating; not telling anyone and risking the health of your teammates is unacceptable.

Please review the attached FAQ and talk to me or Coach Sarah Thompson if you’re ever unsure about whether to come to the pool. I’d much rather you err on the side of caution and have one extra email in my inbox than see someone get seriously sick.

See you tomorrow,
Michael

Michael James Reynolds
Head Coach & Program Director
Harbour City Aquatics Swim Club

metadata.subject: Quick recap of today’s deck briefing at Dalplex

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – safety communications & family schedule

Last updated: May 18, 2022 – saved in Coaching/Protocols/Safety Comms.docx

1. Evening safety calls
- Since May 2022 I’ve been blocking out two evenings a week after the kids are in bed (usually 8:30–10:00 p.m. at 2140 Coburg Road, Apt 3B) for one-on-one calls with parents who seem especially stressed.
- Example week: May 9, 2022 – called Chloe’s pediatrician, Dr. Harris at the IWK Health Centre, for ~40 minutes to clarify how to talk about deck masking and screening for a swimmer who’s immunocompromised post-leukemia. Then debriefed with Emily Reynolds so we were on the same page about language before I spoke with the family.
- Used that situation (fully anonymized) in the May 12, 2022 “Safety Snapshot” email to all Harbour City Aquatics families to explain why we’re strict about masks on the pool deck and honest symptom reporting.

2. FAQ + role modelling
- Need to keep the safety FAQ updated. Include my November 2020 example: positive PCR at Halifax Infirmary, cancelled a full week of masters practices, emailed then-employer and all 14 swimmers I’d coached that week.
- This story landed well when I shared it verbally with the senior group on deck; reinforces that I hold myself to the same standard I’m asking of them.

3. Family logistics
- Coordinate with Emily’s IWK shifts and Liam’s Age Group practices so my evening call blocks don’t clash with bedtime for Liam and Chloe.
- Ask Sarah Thompson to cover one Development session if I need to extend calls on particularly sensitive cases.

metadata.title: Personal planning note – safety communications & family schedule

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Reynolds <m.reynolds@harbourcityaquatics.ca>
To: Megan O’Leary <m.oleary@harbourcityaquatics.ca>
Date: June 20, 2020 9:14 PM
Subject: Junior A email re: gear + wording

Hey Megan,

Thanks again for jumping on Zoom this afternoon to run through the Junior A plan. I know it’s been a long couple of weeks trying to juggle Kinduct check-ins, the new lane allocations, and all the shifting reopening guidelines from the city.

Quick favour for the group email you’re drafting for Sunday night:

When you send the Junior A reminder about returning in July, please don’t highlight the $85 equipment replacement fee in the main text. You can mention that there will be some minor gear costs coming later in the summer, but keep it general and low-key for now.

Context (just between us): on June 18, 2020 I had a private call with Jason Martin about a few things, and he let me know he was recently laid off from his job at the Halifax Shipyard. They’re two months behind on rent at their apartment on Coburg Road, Halifax, and he sounded pretty stressed. I told him we’d find a way to make things work for their daughter and not let money be the deciding factor.

I’ll follow up with the Martins separately about the $85 equipment replacement fee and any other charges so they don’t feel blindsided in a group message. For now, keep the tone of the email encouraging and focused on schedule, safety protocols, and getting the kids back in the water.

Appreciate you being sensitive to this stuff.

Michael
Head Coach & Program Director
Harbour City Aquatics Swim Club

metadata.subject: Junior A email re: gear + wording

artifacts.content:
Date: June 20, 2020, 10:02 PM
From: Michael
To: Emily

Hey, just finished tweaking tomorrow’s emails and wanted to give you a bit of context in case I seem preoccupied.

I had that call on June 18 with Jason Martin – the dad from Junior A whose daughter is in the same lane as Liam sometimes. He finally opened up and said he’d been laid off from the Halifax Shipyard earlier this month. They’re already two months behind on rent at their place on Coburg Road and he sounded pretty shaken.

Tonight I sent a note to Megan O’Leary (our Junior A coach) asking her to soften the wording in the next group email. Specifically, I told her not to spotlight the $85 equipment replacement fee the way we usually do. I don’t want the Martins to feel singled out or embarrassed in front of the rest of the group.

I’m going to work something out with them quietly – maybe a payment plan through the club, or we just comp the fee this cycle and cover it from the hardship fund. It’s not a huge amount for us, but it clearly is for them right now.

Stuff like this makes me glad we’re in a position to help, but it also makes the whole situation around jobs and families feel more real than the headlines. Just wanted to share so you know where my head is at.

Heading to bed soon – 5:15 alarm isn’t getting any later.

M.
